questions.question: Explain the Waterfall model and state two specific project scenarios where it is most suitable to use in software engineering practice.
keyPoints: Linear sequential approach with distinct phases
Suitable for small projects with clear specifications
Ideal when final product is predictable

questions.question: Differentiate between the Waterfall and Incremental models with respect to flexibility, customer involvement, and risk handling.
keyPoints: Waterfall is rigid; Incremental allows changes between increments
Customer involved early in Waterfall but throughout in Incremental
Higher risk in Waterfall; risk distributed across increments in Incremental
Waterfall delivers at end; Incremental delivers in portions

questions.question: Create a detailed comparison table between Waterfall and Incremental models covering at least six parameters. Additionally, provide two real-world project examples where each model would be appropriately applied with justification.
keyPoints: Parameters: approach, requirements, design, testing, delivery, flexibility, customer feedback, risk
Waterfall examples: embedded systems, regulatory software (banking/medical)
Incremental examples: e-commerce platforms, mobile app development
Justification based on stable vs evolving requirements

questions.question: Discuss the concept of hybrid models in software engineering. Explain how the Waterfall and Incremental approaches can be combined to form the Iterative model, and analyze how risk management is handled in such hybrid approaches with an example.
keyPoints: Hybrid models combine strengths of multiple methodologies
Iterative model uses cyclical planning with incremental delivery
Risk reduction through early prototyping and continuous feedback
Example: Spiral model combines iterative Waterfall with risk analysis
